question: Does greater industrial implementation (IEM Part A->B conversion) and land allocation via environmental/forest clearances correlate with land-price increases at the state level -- i.e. do greater transaction volumes bid up the valuation of the underlying asset (land)?
hypothesised_channel: More implemented projects -> more land transactions -> demand-driven appreciation / agglomeration premium -> higher land value. A plausible mechanism, but it competes with three counter-forces listed in 'why_the_sign_may_reverse'.

design.Y: NHB RESIDEX residential House Price Index, HPI@valuation, year-on-year % change, Q3 FY2025-26 (Dec 2025)
X: IEM conversion % (Part B implemented / Part A filed)
design.control: state GSDP FY25 (current prices, Rs lakh cr) as a proxy for economy size / urbanisation
methods: Spearman (rank, robust to the Gurugram/Raipur outliers)

gsdp_vs_price_reference.reading: economy size barely predicts price growth -- so the price signal is not a generic 'big state' effect

headline: The hypothesis is NOT supported in the available data -- the sign is REVERSED. Every industrial-activity metric correlates NEGATIVELY with residential price growth, and the actual land-allocation measure (forest land granted) is strongly negative: Pearson -0.78, strengthening to -0.85 after controlling for GSDP. The most industrially land-intensive states (Chhattisgarh: 8,061 ha forest cleared, 81% IEM conversion -> -8.9% price; Gujarat: 11,209 ECs -> +6.8%) show the weakest price growth, while the price leaders (Haryana/Gurugram +22.8%, Karnataka/Bengaluru +12.7%) are SERVICES/IT-realty metros with low industrial-land intensity.
why_the_sign_may_reverse: SEPARATE SUB-MARKETS: residential HPI appreciation is an urban services/IT phenomenon (Bengaluru, Gurugram, Chennai); heavy-industry and mining land use (Chhattisgarh, industrial belts) sits in different, cheaper geographies. The two barely touch, so a residential index cannot see the industrial-land channel.

ADMINISTERED PRICING: industrial land is largely allotted at STATE AGENCY rate-card prices (RIICO/GIDC/MIDC/IDCO), not market-cleared auctions -- so higher transaction VOLUME does not freely bid up the quoted price the way it would in a market asset.
REVERSE CAUSATION / LOCATION CHOICE: land-hungry heavy industry deliberately locates where land is CHEAP and abundant; low land value is a CAUSE of siting there, not an effect prevented by it.

what_this_can_and_cannot_say.can: In the states with clean single-source price data, industrial-implementation intensity does not co-move positively with the measurable (residential) land-price signal; if anything it co-moves negatively, and the negative sign survives controlling for economy size.
what_this_can_and_cannot_say.cannot: This is NOT proof that industrial transactions fail to raise land value. It is evidence that the price signal we can measure lives in a different sub-market. The user's specific channel -- industrial transaction volume bidding up INDUSTRIAL land -- is untestable with residential HPI.

limitations: n = 8 states (only those with clean single-source NHB Q3 FY26 city values); Gurugram (+22.8%) and Raipur (-8.9%) are high-leverage outliers -- hence Spearman is reported alongside Pearson.
Cross-section, not a panel: cannot separate cause from effect or absorb state fixed effects.
UNIT MISMATCH: Y is RESIDENTIAL house prices; X is INDUSTRIAL activity. City price mapped to whole state.
IEM Part B is self-reported 'implemented'; EC/FC are pipeline snapshots (2022->fetch), not dated flows.
what_would_actually_test_it: DEPENDENT VARIABLE: industrial-plot transaction prices -- state IGR/registration-department circle-rate revisions on industrial land, or IDC/industrial-agency allotment-rate histories (RIICO/GIDC/MIDC rate cards over time). These measure the ASSET the hypothesis is about.
STRUCTURE: a state x year (better, district x year) PANEL with fixed effects, so within-place variation over time -- not cross-state size differences -- identifies the relationship.
TRANSACTION VOLUME: property/industrial-land registration COUNTS and stamp-duty receipts per state-year (RBI State Finances) as the direct 'transaction volume' regressor.
LAGS: land price should respond to implementation with a lag; the panel must allow X to lead Y by 1-3 years.
sources: NHB RESIDEX National Press Release, Q3 FY2025-26 (Dec 2025), HPI@valuation YoY
DPIIT IEM Part A/B state tables (as used across this repo)
state GSDP FY25 current prices (StatisticsTimes/state budgets, approximate, size-control only)
